mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Guatemala on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America (Central America). Guatemala sits between Mexico to the north and Honduras / Belize / El Salvador to the south 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Guatemala?
mapQuestions.answer: Guatemala City (in Spanish, "Ciudad de Guatemala"). The biggest city in Central America, about 3 million people in the metro area.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Guatemala speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish (official), plus over 20 Mayan languages spoken by indigenous peoples (K'iche', Kaqchikel, Mam, Q'eqchi' and more). Around 40% of Guatemalans speak a Mayan language as their first language.

mapQuestions.question: What is the national bird?
mapQuestions.answer: The resplendent quetzal, a brilliant green-and-red rainforest bird with a tail twice as long as its body. The national currency is also called the quetzal.

mapQuestions.question: How volcanic is Guatemala?
mapQuestions.answer: 37 volcanoes, of which 3 are currently active. Volcán de Fuego (the "Volcano of Fire") erupts almost daily with small explosions visible from miles away.

foods.name: Tortillas
foods.description: Thick handmade corn tortillas, slapped between hands in markets, cooked on hot griddles. The Guatemalan staple, eaten with every meal.

foods.name: Pepián
foods.description: A rich brown stew made with tomato, ground pumpkin seeds, sesame, chillies and chicken or beef. Considered Guatemala's national dish. Mayan origin.

foods.name: Tamales
foods.description: Corn-dough parcels filled with chicken, pork, raisins and olives, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed. Eaten especially at Christmas and weekends.

foods.name: Chuchitos
foods.description: Smaller tamales, more compact, often eaten as a snack with hot sauce and cheese. The everyday version.

foods.name: Hot cacao
foods.description: Real Guatemalan hot chocolate made from local cacao with chilli and cinnamon. The Maya invented chocolate as a drink (not as a bar) about 3,000 years ago.

foods.name: Avocado on everything
foods.description: Guatemalan avocados are some of the best on Earth: creamy, deep green, and often eaten just sliced on top of everything.

scavengerItems.label: Mayan ruins at Tikal (giant temples rising above the rainforest canopy)
A woman in a bright woven Mayan "huipil" (handwoven blouse)
An erupting volcano puffing smoke (Fuego near Antigua erupts almost daily)
A Guatemalan coffee farm in the highlands (some of the world's best beans)
A "chicken bus" (a colourful repurposed American school bus, painted to a riot)
Lake Atitlán with three volcanoes around it (Aldous Huxley called it the most beautiful lake in the world)
A Spanish colonial church in Antigua's ruins
Bright street-art murals or hand-woven textile shops
A market stall stacked with corn (Maya culture is built around maize)
The resplendent quetzal (Guatemala's national bird, in the cloud forests)
A misty cloud forest in the highlands
A jade jewellery shop (Maya considered jade more precious than gold)

geoMatches.description: The capital and biggest city, in the south. Modern, busy, sometimes overlooked, but with great museums (Popol Vuh, Ixchel textile museum) and food.
A small UNESCO World Heritage colonial city in the highlands, ringed by three volcanoes. Cobblestone streets, ruined Spanish churches, brightly painted houses.
A spectacular volcanic crater lake with three volcanoes around it and 12 Maya villages on its shores, each with its own distinctive textile patterns and dialect.
A massive Mayan archaeological site deep in the northern rainforest. Six huge temple-pyramids rise above the trees, some over 60 m tall. Howler monkeys roar at dawn.
A mountain market town famous for the biggest Mayan craft market in Central America (Thursdays and Sundays), with hand-woven textiles, jade and ceramics.
A series of natural turquoise pools cascading down limestone tiers in the rainforest. Reached only by 4x4 down rough mountain roads.

senses.smell: Roasting coffee in the highlands, woodsmoke from village kitchens, fresh tortillas slapping a hot griddle, copal (Mayan incense) at a ceremony, sulphur from Pacaya volcano…
senses.hear: Spanish + Mayan languages mixed in markets, "Saqarik!" "¡Hola!", marimba music (the wooden xylophone is Guatemala's national instrument), the rumble of an erupting Fuego volcano in the distance, howler monkeys at dawn in Tikal…
senses.taste: Deep nutty pepián stew, soft warm tortillas with avocado, sweet sticky tamales, hot spiced cacao, fresh-roasted Guatemalan coffee…
senses.feel: Cool highland air (Antigua is 1,530 m up), the bumpy seat of a chicken bus, hand-woven cotton huipiles, the warm waters of Semuc Champey, the soft fur of a quetzal feather…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big was the Maya civilisation?
stories.body: The Maya built one of the most advanced civilisations of the ancient Americas, with hundreds of city-states across what is now Guatemala, southern Mexico, Belize and Honduras, peaking between about 250 and 900 AD. They invented their own writing system (one of only a handful invented from scratch in human history), built temple-pyramids over 60 metres tall (Tikal's Temple IV is one of the tallest pre-Columbian buildings in the Americas), tracked the planet Venus to within seconds, and developed the concept of "zero" as a number independently of the rest of the world. The Maya didn't disappear: there are still 6 million+ Maya people living in Guatemala today, speaking around 20 Maya languages and keeping many traditions alive.

stories.question: Why are the Lake Atitlán villages so different?
stories.body: Around Lake Atitlán, every Maya village has its OWN distinctive woven huipil (women's blouse) pattern, its OWN dialect, its OWN saint and festival. Villages just a few kilometres apart look noticeably different. The patterns are usually thousands of years old, with motifs based on creation stories, agricultural cycles, and the surrounding landscape. By looking at a woman's huipil, locals can often tell exactly which village she comes from. The huipiles take weeks or months to weave by hand on traditional back-strap looms. Tourists buying them are part of how this craft survives economically.
stories.thinkingQuestion: You could tell which village a person comes from just from the pattern on their clothes. What would your "village pattern" look like?

stories.question: How does an active volcano fit into daily life?
stories.body: Just outside the small colonial city of Antigua sits Volcán de Fuego ("Fire Volcano"), one of the most active volcanoes in Central America. It erupts almost EVERY day, in small puffs and bigger explosions. From Antigua you can see the plumes of smoke rising. At night you can sometimes see the orange glow of lava. Locals are completely used to it: people commute, cook dinner, walk their dogs while Fuego rumbles. The neighbouring Volcán Acatenango (which can be hiked overnight) gives spectacular views of Fuego erupting from a safe distance. A big 2018 eruption was tragically deadly, but on most days Fuego is part of the everyday landscape of central Guatemala.

stories.question: What is Guatemalan coffee?
stories.body: Guatemala grows some of the most prized coffee on Earth. The highland soil (rich volcanic ash), the altitude (over 1,500 m), and the cool nights combine to grow beans with high acidity and complex flavours. Guatemala has 8 official coffee regions, each producing slightly different beans: Antigua coffee is smoky and balanced; Huehuetenango is bright and lemony; Atitlán is round and chocolatey. Most coffee is grown on small family farms ("fincas"), often by Maya families. The coffee is hand-picked, sun-dried on raised beds, and shipped around the world. Cafés in Antigua and Guatemala City roast some of the best.

stories.question: How does Guatemala remember its civil war?
stories.body: For 36 years (1960 to 1996), Guatemala lived through a civil war between the army and left-wing rebels, with around 200,000 people killed or "disappeared". The war was especially harsh on rural Maya communities. Peace agreements were signed in 1996. Modern Guatemala has worked hard, painfully, to remember what happened: there are memorials in many villages, museums in Guatemala City (especially CALDH and the Casa de la Memoria), and a peace prize awarded to the Maya activist Rigoberta Menchú in 1992 for her work documenting the war. Many Guatemalans are still rebuilding from those years. Visitors who want to understand the country deeply do well to learn this history.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Guatemala so far?
Tikal, Antigua, Atitlán, Chichi market, which has felt the most "Guatemala"?
Have you seen a volcano puff or rumble? What did it feel like?
If you could only own ONE Guatemalan textile pattern, which would you choose?
Maya civilisation invented its own writing, mathematics and astronomy. Why don't we hear about it more in schools?

wordSearch.hint: Guatemalan foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Resplendent quetzal
animals.description: Guatemala's national bird. Brilliant emerald green and red, with a tail twice as long as its body. In the cloud forests.

animals.name: Scarlet macaw
animals.description: Bright red, yellow and blue parrot in the lowland jungles around Tikal.

animals.name: Howler monkey
animals.description: The loudest land animal on Earth. Their dawn roars can be heard for kilometres through the Tikal rainforest.

animals.name: Blue morpho butterfly
animals.description: Electric-blue wings as big as your hand, fluttering through the rainforest.

animals.name: Boa constrictor
animals.description: Large non-venomous snake of the lowland jungles. Harmless to humans unless trodden on.

animals.name: Jaguar
animals.description: Lives in Guatemala's northern Petén rainforest. Almost impossible to spot, but their tracks turn up in mud.

animals.name: Leaf-cutter ant
animals.description: Long lines of ants carrying cut bits of leaf many times their size, in every Mayan ruin and rainforest path.
